_source: Landscape design principles, Horticulture references, Irrigation design standards, Landscape maintenance best practices
_note: Long-tail landscaping and horticulture knowledge. Plant identification and care, landscape design, irrigation systems, lawn care, pruning, IPM.

hardiness_zones.usda_zones: Zone 1 (-60°F) to Zone 13 (>60°F); based on average annual minimum temperature; determines which plants survive winter
hardiness_zones.zone_selection: Choose plants rated for your zone or colder; zone 6 plant survives in zones 6-10; planting zone 8 plant in zone 6 = likely winter kill
hardiness_zones.microclimates: South-facing walls (warmer), wind-protected areas, heat islands (pavement); can create 1/2 zone difference; use strategically

plant_classification.annuals: Complete life cycle in one year; flower profusely; replant each year; petunias, impatiens, marigolds; bedding plants
plant_classification.perennials: Live 3+ years; die back to ground in winter (herbaceous) or maintain structure (woody); hostas, daylilies, coneflowers
plant_classification.biennials: Two-year cycle; first year foliage, second year flower and die; foxglove, hollyhock, parsley; less common in landscapes
plant_classification.deciduous: Lose leaves in fall; dormant in winter; oaks, maples, most flowering shrubs; seasonal interest
plant_classification.evergreen: Keep foliage year-round; pines, spruces, hollies, boxwood; winter interest but less seasonal change
plant_classification.native_vs_exotic: Native = indigenous to region, adapted to conditions, support local ecosystems; Exotic = non-native, may require more care, some invasive

sun_requirements.full_sun: 6+ hours direct sun; most vegetables, roses, many flowering perennials; south or west exposures
sun_requirements.part_sun_part_shade: 3-6 hours sun; many ornamentals, some vegetables; morning sun + afternoon shade ideal in hot climates
sun_requirements.full_shade: < 3 hours sun or dappled all day; hostas, ferns, astilbes; north side of buildings, under trees
sun_requirements.assessing_site: Observe through day and seasons; tree shade changes (less in spring before leaf-out); buildings block sun at different angles

soil_requirements.ph: Acid <6.5, Neutral 6.5-7.5, Alkaline >7.5; most plants 6.0-7.0; azaleas/blueberries like 4.5-5.5; test with probe or lab; adjust with lime (raise pH) or sulfur (lower pH)
soil_requirements.texture: Sand (drains fast, low nutrients), Silt (moderate), Clay (drains slow, high nutrients); loam = ideal mix of all three
soil_requirements.drainage: Well-drained = water drains within hours; poorly-drained = standing water; dig test hole, fill with water, observe; most plants need well-drained
soil_requirements.organic_matter: Improves all soils; lightens clay, adds nutrients to sand, increases water holding; compost 2-4 inches tilled in or top dressed

design_principles.unity: Cohesive design; repeating elements (plants, materials, colors); connects parts into whole; avoid too much variety
design_principles.balance: Symmetrical (formal, mirror image) or Asymmetrical (informal, equal visual weight but different elements); stable feel
design_principles.proportion_scale: Relative size; plant to house, plant to plant, hardscape to space; 2 ft shrub next to 1-story house (good), next to 3-story (lost)
design_principles.rhythm_repetition: Repeat plants, colors, shapes at intervals; creates unity and movement; 3-5-7 rule (odd numbers more natural)
design_principles.focalization: Focal point draws eye; specimen tree, sculpture, fountain, front door; don't overdo (too many focal points = chaos)
design_principles.simplicity: Limit plant palette; 3-5 plant types for small yard, 5-9 for large; reduce maintenance, enhance unity

layout_planning.site_analysis: Measure property; note existing features (trees, utilities, drainage, views, buildings); sun/shade patterns; soil conditions; access
layout_planning.functional_areas: Public (street view), Private (backyard entertaining), Service (trash, AC unit, storage); separate with plantings or hardscape
layout_planning.circulation: Walkways, driveways, paths; 4-5 ft wide for main walks (two people), 3 ft for side paths; straight = formal, curved = informal
layout_planning.outdoor_rooms: Define spaces with plantings, walls, or grade changes; dining area, play area, garden room; scale to use (8×10 ft minimum for seating)
layout_planning.utilities: Locate before digging (call 811); avoid planting over septic, utilities; plan access for maintenance (HVAC, meters)

plant_selection.layering: Canopy (trees >25 ft), Understory (small trees 10-25 ft), Shrubs (3-10 ft), Perennials (1-3 ft), Groundcover (<1 ft); creates depth
plant_selection.texture: Fine (small leaves, delicate), Medium, Coarse (large leaves, bold); contrast textures for interest; too much of one = monotony
plant_selection.color: Foliage (green shades, variegated, purple, blue), Flowers (seasonal), Bark (winter interest); analogous (next to each other on color wheel) or complementary (opposite)
plant_selection.seasonal_interest: Spring bloom (dogwood, azalea), Summer bloom (crape myrtle, daylily), Fall color (maple, burning bush), Winter structure (evergreens, ornamental grass)
plant_selection.mature_size: CRITICAL: plant for mature size, not current size; foundation plantings often overgrow in 5-10 years; creates maintenance nightmare

hardscape_elements.patios_walkways: Concrete (durable, economical, 4×4 ft control joints), Pavers (brick, concrete pavers, permeable, pattern options), Flagstone (natural, irregular, mortared or dry-laid), Gravel (economical, drains well, needs edging)
hardscape_elements.retaining_walls: Timber (economical, 3-4 ft max height), Segmental block (modular, interlocking, DIY-friendly, 3-6 ft), Stone (natural, expensive, 2-4 ft dry-laid or mortared), Poured concrete (engineered for tall >4 ft); drainage critical (weep holes, gravel backfill)
hardscape_elements.edging: Defines beds; steel (invisible, durable), plastic (economical, visible), stone (natural, labor-intensive), brick (traditional); install flush with soil for mowing
hardscape_elements.mulch: Wood chips (organic, decomposes, needs replacement every 2-3 years, 2-4 inches deep), Bark (longer lasting, decorative), Stone (permanent, no nutrients, retains heat), Rubber (controversial, doesn't decompose, expensive)

sprinkler_types.spray_heads: Fixed pattern; 5-15 ft radius; residential lawns and beds; 1.5 inches/hr precipitation rate; relatively uniform coverage
sprinkler_types.rotors: Rotating stream; 15-50 ft radius; large lawns, commercial; 0.5 inches/hr; wind-resistant; adjustable arc (40-360°)
sprinkler_types.drip_emitters: Low volume; delivers water at roots; 0.5-2 GPH per emitter; beds, shrubs, trees; very efficient (95%+), minimal evaporation/runoff
sprinkler_types.bubblers: Low-volume stream; tree basins; 0.5-4 GPH; flood small area; economical for spot watering
sprinkler_types.micro_spray: Fine mist; small beds, tight spaces; 5-12 inch radius; 0.25-1 GPH; prevent clogging with filtration

system_design.zoning: Separate zones by: plant water needs (lawn vs shrubs), sun exposure (sunny vs shade), head type (sprays vs rotors = different precipitation rates), slope (runoff control)
system_design.head_spacing: Head-to-head coverage; spray radius overlaps to next head; example: 15 ft spray heads spaced 15 ft apart (triangular or square pattern); prevents dry spots
system_design.water_pressure: Residential 40-80 PSI typical; too high damages heads (use pressure regulator); too low reduces coverage; test at water source (hose bib)
system_design.flow_rate: GPM (gallons per minute); each head has GPM rating; zone total GPM must not exceed available GPM (test with bucket: time to fill 5 gal = GPM)
system_design.pipe_sizing: 1" mainline typical residential; 3/4" laterals; 1/2" for drip; size for flow and pressure loss; 3-5 ft/sec velocity prevents noise/erosion

installation.trenching: 6-8 inches deep for lateral lines (below frost line in cold climates, or blow out system); 12+ inches for mainline; avoid tree roots
installation.pipe: PVC Schedule 40 (pressure-rated, white/grey); swing joints (reduces break risk from mowing/settling); glue per manufacturer (clean, primer, cement, hold 30 sec)
installation.valves: Solenoid valves (electric, controller-operated); one per zone; install in valve box; anti-siphon or backflow preventer required by code
installation.wire: 18-gauge multi-strand; color-coded; one common (white) to all valves, one hot wire per valve (colors); bury 6-12 inches; waterproof connections (wire nuts + silicone grease + waterproof cap)
installation.controller: Timer; sets schedule (days, start times, run times per zone); rain sensor shuts off system when raining (code requirement many areas); WiFi smart controllers adjust for weather

scheduling_efficiency.watering_depth: Lawns 6 inches deep (6-12 inches roots), Shrubs 12 inches, Trees 12-24 inches; water infrequently and deeply (encourages deep roots)
scheduling_efficiency.frequency: Clay soil: 1-2× per week; Loam: 2-3×; Sand: 3-5×; adjust for season (more in summer, less in spring/fall); new plantings more frequent until established
scheduling_efficiency.time_of_day: Early morning best (4-8 AM); less wind, less evaporation, foliage dries during day (reduces disease); evening OK but foliage stays wet (disease risk); midday wastes water (evaporation)
scheduling_efficiency.run_time_calculation: Determine precipitation rate (catch can test: run zone 15 min, measure depth in cans, multiply by 4 = inches per hour); divide desired depth by precip rate = hours
scheduling_efficiency.seasonal_adjust: 100% summer; 75% spring/fall; 50% or off in winter (dormant grass, rainfall sufficient); smart controllers auto-adjust using weather data
scheduling_efficiency.auditing: Annual checkup; run each zone, look for broken heads, clogged emitters, misdirected spray, leaks; catch can test for uniformity; adjust heads and timing as needed

cool_season.kentucky_bluegrass: Dense, dark green; spreads by rhizomes (self-repairs); full sun; high maintenance; zones 3-7; slow to establish
cool_season.perennial_ryegrass: Quick germination (5-7 days); bright green; bunch-type (doesn't spread); tolerates traffic; overseeding lawns
cool_season.tall_fescue: Drought-tolerant; deep roots; bunch-type; low maintenance; tolerates heat better than others; coarse texture
cool_season.fine_fescues: Shade-tolerant; low maintenance; fine texture; low water needs; poor traffic tolerance; mix with bluegrass

warm_season.bermudagrass: Aggressive; spreads by stolons and rhizomes; full sun; turns brown in winter; high maintenance; zones 7-10; drought-tolerant
warm_season.zoysiagrass: Dense; slow-growing; tolerates shade; low maintenance once established; slow to green up in spring; zones 6-9
warm_season.st_augustine: Shade-tolerant; coarse texture; spreads by stolons; low mowing height tolerance; zones 8-10; chinch bugs susceptible
warm_season.centipedegrass: Low maintenance; acidic soil; slow-growing; light green; 'lazy man's grass'; zones 7-10; low fertility needs

turfgrass_types.selection: Cool season for northern US; warm season for southern; transition zone (6-7) = challenge, choose heat-tolerant cool or cold-tolerant warm

mowing.height: Cool season: 2.5-3.5 inches (taller in summer heat); Warm season: 1-2.5 inches (bermuda 0.5-1.5, zoysia 1-2, St. Augustine 2.5-3); taller = deeper roots, drought tolerance, weed suppression
mowing.frequency: One-third rule: never remove >1/3 of blade height in one mowing; if 3 inch target, mow when reaches 4.5 inches; more frequent in spring (rapid growth)
mowing.pattern: Vary pattern each time; prevents soil compaction, ruts, grain (grass leans one direction); stripe with rear roller if desired
mowing.blade_sharpness: Sharpen every 25-50 hours use (residential 2-3× per year); dull blade tears grass (brown tips, disease entry point); sharp blade clean cut
mowing.clipping_management: Mulch clippings (return nutrients, nitrogen); bag if excessive (>1/3 height removed, clumping) or diseased; clippings don't cause thatch (roots/stems do)

fertilization.npk_ratios: N-P-K = Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium; N = green growth, P = roots, K = disease resistance/stress tolerance; lawn fertilizers high in N (e.g., 29-0-4, 20-5-10)
fertilization.cool_season_schedule: Light spring (1 lb N per 1000 sq ft), heavy fall (1.5-2 lbs); fall most important (root growth); 3-4 lbs N total per year
fertilization.warm_season_schedule: Apply when actively growing (late spring through summer); 3-4 applications; 3-5 lbs N total per year; nothing in winter (dormant)
fertilization.application: Broadcast spreader (rotary or drop); calibrate (measure output); half-rate in two directions (prevent streaking); water in after (especially quick-release)
fertilization.organic_vs_synthetic: Synthetic (quick-release, predictable, cheaper, can burn); Organic (slow-release, builds soil, expensive, bulkier); milorganite popular organic

watering.amount: 1-1.5 inches per week (including rain); measure with rain gauge or tuna cans; supplement as needed
watering.technique: Deep and infrequent (6 inches deep, 1-2× per week) better than shallow daily (encourages deep roots, drought tolerance)
watering.signs_of_need: Footprints remain (grass doesn't spring back), bluish tint, leaf blades fold/roll; water before severe wilting
watering.new_seed: Keep surface moist until germination (water daily or 2×daily lightly); after germination, water less frequently but deeper; transition to normal schedule after 3-4 weeks

aeration_dethatching.core_aeration: Pulls soil plugs 2-3 inches deep; relieves compaction; increases water/air penetration; do when actively growing (spring/fall cool season, late spring/summer warm season); heavy traffic areas annually, others every 2-3 years
aeration_dethatching.thatch: Layer of dead roots/stems between soil and green grass; >0.5 inch is problematic (water/nutrient barrier, disease harbor)
aeration_dethatching.dethatching: Power rake or vertical mower; pulls up thatch; stressful to lawn; do when grass can recover (actively growing); every few years as needed, not annually
aeration_dethatching.overseeding: After aeration or dethatching; spreads new seed; fills in bare spots; improves variety genetics; cool season grass in fall, warm season in spring; keep moist until established

weed_control.pre_emergent: Prevents weed seeds from germinating; crabgrass preventer (apply early spring, soil 55°F for 3 days); doesn't kill existing weeds; lasts 2-4 months; can't seed for 8-12 weeks after application
weed_control.post_emergent: Kills existing weeds; selective (kills weeds, not grass) or non-selective (kills everything, Roundup); broadleaf killers (2,4-D, dicamba) for dandelions, clover; apply when weeds actively growing, not drought-stressed
weed_control.spot_treatment: Hand-pull, dig out (dandelion digger), or spray individual weeds; prevents waste and reduces chemical use; effective for light infestations
weed_control.cultural_control: Dense healthy lawn crowds out weeds; proper mowing height, fertilization, watering reduces weed pressure; weed prevention > weed killing

timing.dormant_pruning: Late winter before spring growth; most trees/shrubs; easy to see structure; vigorous regrowth
timing.after_flowering: Spring bloomers (forsythia, lilac, azalea); bloom on old wood; prune immediately after bloom; next year's buds set in summer
timing.summer_pruning: Limit growth; remove water sprouts, suckers; formal hedges; slow regrowth compared to dormant pruning
timing.avoid_fall: Promotes new growth that won't harden off before winter (freeze damage); exception: remove dead/diseased anytime

pruning_principles.the_3_ds: Dead (no green under bark), Diseased (cankers, discoloration), Damaged (broken, rubbing, crossing) branches; always remove regardless of season
pruning_principles.thinning_vs_heading: Thinning = remove entire branch at junction (natural look, opens interior); Heading = cut mid-branch (stimulates dense regrowth, formal hedges)
pruning_principles.branch_collar: Swollen area where branch meets trunk; cut just outside collar (don't leave stub, don't cut flush); collar heals wound naturally

trees.young_trees: Structural pruning; select scaffold branches (main framework); remove competing leaders, narrow crotch angles (<45°), low branches as tree grows
trees.mature_trees: Minimal pruning; remove dead/damaged; thin to reduce wind resistance (storms); raise canopy (clearance); reduce if too large (expensive, stressful)
trees.three_cut_method: Large branches: 1) undercut 12-18" from trunk (prevents bark tear), 2) top cut farther out (removes weight), 3) final cut at branch collar

shrubs.renewal_pruning: Overgrown shrubs; cut 1/3 of oldest stems to ground each year; in 3 years, completely renewed; maintains size and vigor
shrubs.rejuvenation_pruning: Cut entire shrub to 6-12 inches in late winter; vigorous regrowth; works for many shrubs (spirea, barberry) but not all (rhododendron poor response)
shrubs.shearing: Formal hedges; hedge shears or power trimmer; wider at base than top (sunlight to lower branches); timing varies by plant

roses.hybrid_teas_floribundas: Prune hard in spring; remove dead/diseased; cut to 6-8 strong canes, 12-18 inches tall; 45° angle above outward-facing bud
roses.climbing_roses: Minimal pruning; remove dead; tie to support; bloom on old wood (don't prune after flowering); shape as needed
roses.shrub_roses: Light pruning; remove dead/diseased; shape; bloom on new wood (can prune spring or after bloom)

fertilization.trees_shrubs: Slow-release fertilizer early spring; 10-10-10 or specific formulation (acid-loving plants 10-5-4); broadcast around drip line, not trunk; 1-2 lbs per inch trunk diameter
fertilization.perennials: Balanced fertilizer (10-10-10) or bloom-boosting (5-10-5) early spring; side-dress or liquid feed during growing season; avoid high-N late summer (promotes soft growth)
fertilization.acid_loving: Azaleas, rhododendrons, blueberries, hollies; acidic fertilizer (ammonium sulfate) or sulfur to lower pH; iron for chlorosis (yellowing leaves, green veins)
fertilization.avoid_overfertilization: Burns plants; excessive vegetative growth (weak, disease-prone); environmental runoff; follow label rates; less is more

integrated_pest_management.monitoring: Scout regularly; identify pest (insect, disease, weed); determine threshold (how much damage is acceptable before treatment); not every bug requires pesticide
integrated_pest_management.cultural_controls: Sanitation (remove diseased leaves, prune dead wood), proper watering/fertilization (healthy plants resist pests), plant selection (disease-resistant varieties), crop rotation
integrated_pest_management.mechanical_physical: Hand-pick (Japanese beetles, caterpillars), barriers (row covers, tree wraps), traps (pheromone, sticky), pruning out (bagworms, tent caterpillars), water spray (aphids)
integrated_pest_management.biological: Beneficial insects (ladybugs eat aphids, parasitic wasps), Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis, kills caterpillars, organic), nematodes (kill soil grubs)
integrated_pest_management.chemical: Last resort; choose least-toxic effective option (insecticidal soap, horticultural oil, neem); target specific pest; follow label exactly (rate, timing, PPE, re-entry); rotate modes of action (resistance prevention)

spring.early: Remove winter mulch from perennials; prune summer-blooming shrubs; dormant oil spray (scale, mites); pre-emergent herbicide; divide perennials; test soil
spring.mid: Fertilize lawn (light) and beds; mulch beds; plant annuals after last frost; start mower maintenance; edge beds; begin mowing (raise deck initially)
spring.late: Deadhead spring bulbs (remove flower, leave foliage 6 weeks); monitor for pests; adjust irrigation for seasonal needs; plant warm-season vegetables

summer.tasks: Water deeply and infrequently; deadhead annuals and perennials (encourages rebloom); monitor for pests/diseases; fertilize annuals every 2-4 weeks; mow at higher height (heat stress); prune spring-blooming shrubs after bloom; weed control
summer.heat_stress: Water early morning; mulch to conserve moisture; avoid pruning and fertilizing during extreme heat; provide shade (shade cloth) for sensitive plants

fall.early: Fertilize lawn (heavy, most important application); overseed cool-season lawns; plant spring bulbs; divide perennials; plant trees/shrubs (good root establishment before winter)
fall.mid: Rake leaves (mulch or compost, don't pile on lawn); last mow (cool season continues, warm season slows); clean and store irrigation (blow out lines in freezing climates); cut back perennials or leave for winter interest
fall.late: Protect tender plants (mulch, burlap wrap); drain hoses and fountains; final leaf cleanup; tool maintenance; apply winter fertilizer (high K for hardiness); anti-desiccant spray on evergreens (dry winter areas)

winter.tasks: Plan next season; order seeds/plants; maintain tools; monitor for animal damage (deer, rabbits); brush snow off evergreens (prevent breakage); apply dormant oil spray (mild days); prune fruit trees; inspect for winter damage after storms

estimating_pricing.labor_rates: $25-50/hr per person for maintenance; $40-80/hr for installation; adjust for region, skill, overhead; calculate break-even, add markup 30-50%
estimating_pricing.maintenance_pricing: Per visit (mow, trim, edge, blow = $30-60 for small yard, $60-150 large); monthly contract (stability); per hour (small jobs)
estimating_pricing.installation_pricing: Materials × 2-3 for total price (materials + labor + overhead + profit); or estimate hours × labor rate + materials × markup (1.3-1.5)
estimating_pricing.bidding_jobs: Walk site; measure; note access, obstacles, soil, disposal; list materials with quantities; estimate hours; present professional proposal; follow up

equipment.mowing: 21" push mower ($200-500, small yards); 32-48" walk-behind ($1500-4000, residential); 48-60" zero-turn ($3000-8000+, commercial/large); trailer ($500-2000)
equipment.power_tools: String trimmer, edger, blower (backpack $300-600), hedge trimmer ($150-400), chainsaw ($200-600); 2-stroke vs 4-stroke vs battery; commercial grade for daily use
equipment.hand_tools: Shovels, rakes (leaf, garden, landscape), pruners (hand, loppers, pole saw), wheelbarrow, digging bar, hoes; quality tools last
equipment.maintenance: Sharpen mower blades regularly; change oil, air filter, spark plugs per schedule; clean equipment after use; winter storage (stabilizer in fuel, drain carbs); repair > replace for commercial equipment

licensing_insurance.business_license: Required in most areas; city/county; $50-500; annual renewal
licensing_insurance.pesticide_applicator_license: Required to apply pesticides commercially; testing, CEUs; state-specific; serious penalties for unlicensed application
licensing_insurance.liability_insurance: General liability $1-2M coverage; protects against property damage, injury; $500-2000/year; required by many commercial clients
licensing_insurance.workers_comp: Required if employees; covers injury on job; rates vary by state and payroll; expensive but necessary
licensing_insurance.vehicle_insurance: Commercial auto for trucks/trailers; higher limits than personal; covers equipment in transit

client_management.contracts: Written agreement; scope of work, price, schedule, payment terms, cancellation policy; protects both parties; reduces disputes
client_management.scheduling: Route planning (minimize drive time); weekly maintenance accounts vs on-call; weather delays (build in buffer); software (Jobber, LMN)
client_management.communication: Confirm appointments; update on delays; text/email for efficiency; professional appearance and demeanor
client_management.invoicing_payment: Invoice promptly; payment terms (due upon receipt, net 15, net 30); accept credit cards (fee 2-3% but convenience); autopay for recurring clients; late fees (clearly stated in contract)
